🐵
Africa

Olive Baboon

Papio anubis
Status Least Concern
Habitat Savannahs, Forests, Mountains
Diet Omnivore
Lifespan 25-30 years
Weight 13-27 kg (male), 10-15 kg (female)
×

The olive baboon (Papio anubis), also known as the Anubis baboon, represents one of Africa's most adaptable and widespread primates. These highly intelligent, social monkeys inhabit diverse environments across East Africa, from savannas to forests and even mountainous regions. With their distinctive dog-like muzzles, close-set eyes, and olive-green tinted coats, they form complex societies characterized by intricate social hierarchies and cooperative behaviors. As opportunistic omnivores, they display remarkable foraging intelligence, using tools and solving problems to access food. Their adaptability has allowed them to thrive in human-modified landscapes, though this often brings them into conflict with farmers and communities.

Habitat & Range

Olive baboons demonstrate remarkable habitat flexibility, inhabiting savannas, grasslands, forests, and mountainous regions across sub-Saharan Africa. In East Africa, they are found from sea level to 3,000 meters elevation, including the Serengeti plains, Amboseli basin, and Ethiopian highlands. They require access to water, sleeping cliffs or trees for nighttime refuge, and diverse food sources. Unlike many primates, they have successfully adapted to human-modified landscapes, often found near agricultural areas, tourist facilities, and even urban edges. Their adaptability stems from generalist feeding habits and social flexibility.

Behavior & Social Structure

Olive baboons live in large multi-male, multi-female troops averaging 30-80 individuals, though groups of 150+ occur. Strict dominance hierarchies govern both male and female social relationships, with rank determining access to food, mates, and grooming partners. Males typically leave their birth troop at maturity and transfer between groups, while females remain lifelong members of their natal troop. They display sophisticated social behaviors including reconciliation after conflicts, coalition formation, and cooperative infant care. Communication involves complex vocalizations, facial expressions, and body postures. They are diurnal, foraging during the day and sleeping in trees or on cliffs at night for protection from predators.

Diet & Nutrition

As opportunistic omnivores, olive baboons consume over 200 different food types including grasses, seeds, fruits, leaves, insects, small mammals, birds, and eggs. Their diet varies seasonally and by habitat, with savanna populations eating more grasses and roots while forest populations focus on fruits and insects. They are skilled foragers, digging for roots and tubers, stripping bark, and using tools to access difficult foods. Near human settlements, they readily exploit crops, garbage, and handouts, leading to conflict. Their diverse diet and foraging intelligence contribute significantly to their ecological success and adaptability across varied environments.

Key Facts

  • Most widespread baboon species across Africa
  • Live in large troops of up to 150 individuals
  • Complex social hierarchy with dominant male leaders
  • Highly intelligent: use tools and solve problems
  • Opportunistic omnivores eating over 200 food types
  • Infants ride on mother's back for first 6 months

Conservation Status

Classified as Least Concern by the IUCN Red List due to their wide distribution and adaptability. Olive baboons remain abundant across East Africa, with populations estimated in the hundreds of thousands. Their ability to thrive in diverse habitats, including human-modified landscapes, has buffered them from many threats affecting other primates. However, they face persecution as crop raiders in agricultural areas, and habitat loss in some regions affects local populations. In protected areas like the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem, Amboseli, and Tsavo, they maintain stable populations. Conservation focuses on mitigating human-wildlife conflict rather than species protection.

Primary Threats

  • Human-wildlife conflict and persecution as crop raiders
  • Habitat loss in some regions
  • Hunting for bushmeat in some areas
  • Capture for illegal pet trade
  • Disease transmission from humans and domestic animals

Related Species

🐘 African Elephant 🦁 Lion 🦒 Maasai Giraffe

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Olive Baboon?

Olive Baboons typically weigh 13-27 kg (male), 10-15 kg (female) and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Savannahs, Forests, Mountains.

Are Olive Baboons endangered?

The Olive Baboon is currently classified as Least Concern by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Least Concern by the IUCN Red List due to their wide distribution and adaptability. Olive baboons remain abundant across East Africa, wi...

What do Olive Baboons eat?

Olive Baboons are omnivore. As opportunistic omnivores, olive baboons consume over 200 different food types including grasses, seeds, fruits, leaves...

How long do Olive Baboons live?

In the wild, Olive Baboons typically live for 25-30 years. Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
